- [Proportion of ...]{.des}: A descriptive label for what the population proportion represents  

**(b)** State the null and alternative hypotheses.

**(c)** Carry out the test using the **critical region (rejection region) approach**:  
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;(i) Specify the critical region.  
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;(ii) Identify $x_{\text{obs}}$ and determine whether you reject or fail to reject $H_0$.

**(d)** Carry out the test using the **p-value approach**:  
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;(i) Specify the $H_a$ support.  
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;(ii) State the p-value and determine whether you reject or fail to reject $H_0$.

**(e)** State your conclusion in the context of the problem.
---

1. **Arizona Marijuana Legalization**
In Problem 1 (Arizona Marijuana Legalization) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$ is
the true proportion of Arizona registered voters who support marijuana
legalization. Do Arizona registered voters differ from Colorado residents
in their support for marijuana legalization? The sample consisted of
$n = 779$ voters, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 390$ favored legalization.
Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

2. **Australian Unemployment Outlook**
In Problem 2 (Australian Unemployment Outlook) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$ is
the true proportion of Australians in November 1997 who believed
unemployment would increase. Did Australian sentiment about unemployment
shift between July and November of 1997? The sample consisted of
$n = 631$ Australians, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 284$ believed
unemployment would increase. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

3. **Arkansas Identity Theft Complaints**
In Problem 3 (Arkansas Identity Theft Complaints) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of consumer complaints in Arkansas that were for
identity theft. Was the proportion of identity theft complaints in Arkansas
greater than the national figure of 23%? The sample consisted of
$n = 3{,}482$ complaints, of which $x_{\text{obs}} = 1{,}601$ were for
identity theft. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

4. **Alaska Identity Theft Complaints**
In Problem 4 (Alaska Identity Theft Complaints) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of consumer complaints in Alaska that were for
identity theft. Was Alaska's proportion of identity theft complaints less
than the national proportion of 23%? The sample consisted of
$n = 1{,}432$ complaints, of which $x_{\text{obs}} = 321$ were for
identity theft. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

5. **Kennedy Assassination Conspiracy Belief**
In Problem 5 (Kennedy Assassination Conspiracy Belief) of @sec-stage-hw,
$p$ is the true proportion of American adults in 2013 who believed there
was a conspiracy behind President Kennedy's assassination. Is there evidence
that American belief in a conspiracy behind President Kennedy's assassination
changed between 2001 and 2013? The sample consisted of $n = 1{,}039$
adults, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 634$ believed in a conspiracy.
Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

6. **Autism Spectrum Disorder in Arizona**
In Problem 6 (Autism Spectrum Disorder in Arizona) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of children in Arizona diagnosed with ASD. Is the
rate of ASD diagnosis among children in Arizona greater than the national
rate of 1 in 88 children? The sample consisted of $n = 32{,}601$ children,
of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 507$ were diagnosed with ASD. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

7. **Graduate Degree Attainment: GSS2024**
In Problem 7 (Graduate Degree Attainment: GSS2024) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of U.S. adults who hold a graduate degree. Does the GSS2024 data provide evidence that the proportion of U.S. adults who hold a graduate degree differs from the 15% reported by the U.S. Census Bureau? Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

<div tabindex="0" style="width:85%; margin:auto;">

![Rguroo dataset logo](Rguroo_Icons/Rguroo_repo_logo.png) The dataset for
this exercise is available in the Rguroo dataset repository [Kozak]{.repo},
with the dataset name [GSS2024]{.data}.

**Code book for Dataset GSS2024 is given [here](#code-book-gss2024).**

</div>

## A Closer Look at Critical Regions and P-values ** {#sec-closer-look-cr-pvalue}

::: {.callout-note}
This section is optional. The material here is not required for performing hypothesis tests, but it is highly recommended for readers who want a firmer conceptual grasp of what is happening behind the procedures.
:::


In the previous sections, you learned two equivalent approaches for testing a hypothesis about a population proportion: the **critical region approach** and the **p-value approach**. In both approaches, we computed probabilities for our decisions using the boundary value of the null hypothesis, and we relied on Rguroo's hypothesis testing module to produce the critical region or the p-value automatically.

This section is intended to deepen your understanding of *why* these procedures work the way they do. Specifically, we address two questions:

1. **Why do we use the boundary value of $H_0$ when computing probabilities required for our decisions?** The null hypothesis in many tests is a statement such as $H_0: p \leq p_0$ or $H_0: p \geq p_0$, which encompasses many possible values of $p$. Yet we always use the single boundary value $p_0$ in our calculations. There is a principled reason for this, and understanding it clarifies what a hypothesis test is really doing.

2. **How are critical regions and p-values actually obtained from a probability distribution?** Rguroo's hypothesis testing module returns these quantities directly, but doing so hides the underlying probability calculation. By using Rguroo's **probability calculator** instead, we can see exactly which area under a sampling distribution corresponds to the critical region or the p-value. 


### Why Use the boundary value of $p$ in $H_0$? ** {#sec-boundary-value}

In this section we explain why we use the boundary value of the null hypothesis when determining the critical region (or, equivalently, when computing the p-value). A detailed account of this topic, including worked examples and the underlying mathematics, is presented in a paper by [Jamshidian and Jamshidian (2026)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/26939169.2025.2610942). Here, we present the idea more informally, through examples.

Recall that a Type I error occurs when we reject $H_0$ even though $H_0$ is in fact true. The probability of making a Type I error depends on two things: the **critical region** we choose, and the **true value of $p$** under $H_0$. When $H_0$ is composite — for example, $H_0: p \leq p_0$ — there are infinitely many values of $p$ consistent with $H_0$, and in principle each of these values gives rise to its own Type I error probability.

We begin by showing how the probability of a Type I error is computed for various choices of the critical region and for various values of $p$ permitted by $H_0$. As we will see, the boundary value $p = p_0$ plays a special role: it is the value at which this probability is largest, and controlling the error rate there automatically controls it everywhere else in $H_0$.

:::{#exm-youtube-type1}
## Daily YouTube Use Among Teens (continued)

Recall @exm-youtube-ppss where a school district claimed that more than 70% of 
students in its district use YouTube daily, and a random sample of $n = 20$ 
students was selected. The null and alternative hypotheses are:
$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p \leq 0.70 \\
H_a: p > 0.70
\end{cases}
$$

Consider the critical region: Reject $H_0$ if $X \geq 17$.

For this critical region, compute $P(\text{Type I error})$ when 

a. $p = 0.60$
b. $p = 0.65$
c. $p = 0.70$.

In each case, interpret the probability in the context of the problem.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
a\. Since the sample size is $n = 20$ and $p = 0.60$, we have 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(20, 0.60)$. Using Rguroo's probability calculator, 
we compute
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$$
$$= P(X \geq 17 \mid p = 0.60).$$

Click to expand the box below to see how to calculate the probability of Type I error.

:::{#calculating-typeI-error .callout-note appearance="simple" 
collapse="true" icon="none" 
title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px 
style='vertical-align:middle;'} Calculating Type I Error Probability"}

1. Open the **Probability-Simulation** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. From the [Probability]{.dpd} dropdown, select 
[Probability Calculator --> Discrete]{.fun}.
3. (Optional) In the `Calculation` textbox, type a name for the calculation 
(e.g., `Type I error`).  
4. In the [Distribution]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Binomial]{.fun}.
5. In the [No. of Trials]{.des} textbox, enter [20]{.typein}.
6. In the [Prob. of Success]{.des} textbox, enter [0.60]{.typein}.
7. In the next dropdown menu, select [Above]{.dpd}, and in the textbox next 
to it, enter [17]{.typein}.
8. (Optional) Select the [Graph]{.des} checkbox to see a graph showing 
calculation details.
9. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) 
to see the result.
10. (Optional) In the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) textbox, enter 
the name [TypeI_Error]{.typein} to save this calculation to your Rguroo 
account.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout appearance="simple" collapse="true" 
icon="none" title="Click here to see the Rguroo dialog"}
![Probability calculator dialog for computing Type I error probability
](Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/Prob_Type_I_error_dialog.png){width=500px}
:::

::::

@fig-youtube-type1a shows the Rguroo Probability Calculator output for 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(20, 0.60)$, giving 

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \geq 17\mid p=0.60) \approx 0.016.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of students in the district who use YouTube 
daily is $p = 0.60$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever 
$X \geq 17$ in a sample of $n = 20$ students, then there is only a $1.6\%$ 
chance of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding that 
more than 70% of students in the district use YouTube daily.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-cap: "Binomial distribution for $X \\sim \\text{Binomial}(n = 20, p = 0.60)$ showing the Type I error probability as the upper-tail area $P(X \\geq 17) = 0.016$."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-youtube-type1a
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/Prob_Type_I_Error_graph.png")
```

b\. To compute the probability of Type I error for the critical region 
$X \geq 17$ when $p = 0.65$, we follow the same steps as in 
the [part (a)](#calculating-typeI-error), but enter [0.65]{.typein} in the 
[Prob. of Success]{.des} box. The Rguroo output gives 

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \geq 17\mid p=0.65) \approx 0.044.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of students in the district who use YouTube 
daily is $p = 0.65$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever 
$X \geq 17$ in a sample of $n = 20$ students, then there is approximately a 
$4.4\%$ chance of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding 
that more than 70% of students in the district use YouTube daily.

c\. Entering [0.70]{.typein} in the [Prob. of Success]{.des} box gives

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \geq 17\mid p=0.70) \approx 0.107.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of students in the district who use YouTube 
daily is $p = 0.70$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever 
$X \geq 17$ in a sample of $n = 20$ students, then there is approximately a 
$10.7\%$ chance of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding 
that more than 70% of students in the district use YouTube daily.
:::

:::{#exm-physical-inactivity-type1}
## Physical Inactivity Among Adults (continued)

Recall @exm-physical-inactivity-ppss where a national health agency claimed 
that fewer than 35% of adults in its country are physically inactive, and a 
random sample of $n = 50$ adults was selected. The null and alternative 
hypotheses are:
$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p \geq 0.35 \\
H_a: p < 0.35
\end{cases}
$$

Consider the critical region: Reject $H_0$ if $X \leq 13$.

For this critical region, compute $P(\text{Type I error})$ when 

a. $p = 0.35$ 
b. $p = 0.40$
c. $p = 0.50$.

In each case, interpret the probability in the context of the problem.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a\. Since the sample size is $n = 50$ and $p = 0.35$, we have 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(50, 0.35)$. Using Rguroo's probability calculator, 
we compute
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$$
$$= P(X \leq 13 \mid p = 0.35).$$

Click to expand the box below to see how to calculate the probability of 
Type I error.

:::{#calculating-typeI-errorb .callout-note appearance="simple" 
collapse="true" icon="none" 
title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px 
style='vertical-align:middle;'} Calculating Type I Error Probability"}

1. Open the **Probability-Simulation** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. From the [Probability]{.dpd} dropdown, select 
[Probability Calculator --> Discrete]{.fun}.
3. (Optional) In the `Calculation` textbox, type a name for the calculation 
(e.g., `Type I error`).  
4. In the [Distribution]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Binomial]{.fun}.
5. In the [No. of Trials]{.des} textbox, enter [50]{.typein}.
6. In the [Prob. of Success]{.des} textbox, enter [0.35]{.typein}.
7. In the next dropdown menu, select [Below]{.dpd}, and in the textbox next 
to it, enter [13]{.typein}.
8. (Optional) Select the [Graph]{.des} checkbox to see a graph showing 
calculation details.
9. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) 
to see the result.
10. (Optional) In the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) textbox, enter 
the name [Type I Error Inactivity]{.typein} to save this calculation to your 
Rguroo account.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout appearance="simple" collapse="true" 
icon="none" title="Click here to see the Rguroo dialog"}
![Probability calculator dialog for computing Type I error probability
](Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/TypeI_Error_Inactivity_dialog.png){width=500px}
:::
:::

@fig-youtube-type1b shows the Rguroo Probability Calculator output for 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(50, 0.35)$, giving 

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \leq 13\mid p=0.35) \approx 0.1163.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of inactive adults in the population is 
$p = 0.35$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever $X \leq 13$ 
in a sample of $n = 50$ adults, then there is approximately an $11.63\%$ chance 
of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding that fewer than 
35% of adults in the population are physically inactive.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-cap: "Binomial distribution for $X \\sim \\text{Binomial}(n = 50, p = 0.35)$ showing the Type I error probability as the lower-tail area $P(X \\leq 13) = 0.1163$."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-youtube-type1b
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/Type I Error Inactivity_graph.png")
```

b\. Entering [0.40]{.typein} in the [Prob. of Success]{.des} box gives

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \leq 13\mid p=0.40) \approx 0.028.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of inactive adults in the population is 
$p = 0.40$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever $X \leq 13$ 
in a sample of $n = 50$ adults, then there is approximately a $2.8\%$ chance 
of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding that fewer than 
35% of adults in the population are physically inactive.

c\. Entering [0.50]{.typein} in the [Prob. of Success]{.des} box gives

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \leq 13\mid p=0.50) \approx 0.00046.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of inactive adults in the population is 
$p = 0.50$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ whenever $X \leq 13$ 
in a sample of $n = 50$ adults, then there is approximately a $0.046\%$ chance 
of committing a Type I error; that is, incorrectly concluding that fewer than 
35% of adults in the population are physically inactive.

::::

:::{#exm-social-media-news-type1}
## Social Media as a Primary News Source (continued)

Recall @exm-social-media-news-ppss where a university administration wanted 
to determine whether the proportion of students who get most of their news from 
social media differs from 36%, and a random sample of $n = 15$ students was 
selected. The null and alternative hypotheses are:
$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p = 0.36 \\
H_a: p \ne 0.36
\end{cases}
$$

Note that the two-sided case is slightly more complicated than the one-sided 
cases above: since the alternative hypothesis claims that $p$ differs from 
$0.36$ in either direction, evidence against $H_0$ can come from either tail 
of the distribution.

Consider the critical region: Reject $H_0$ if $X \leq 3$ or $X \geq 8$.

For this critical region, compute $P(\text{Type I error})$ when $p = 0.36$.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

Since $H_0$ specifies only a single value $p = 0.36$, we have 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(15, 0.36)$. The probability of Type I error is
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$$
$$= P(X \leq 3 \text{ or } X \geq 8 \mid p = 0.36).$$

Click to expand the box below to see how to calculate the probability of 
Type I error.

:::{#calculating-typeI-errorc .callout-note appearance="simple" 
collapse="true" icon="none" 
title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px 
style='vertical-align:middle;'} Calculating Type I Error Probability"}

1. Open the **Probability-Simulation** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. From the [Probability]{.dpd} dropdown, select 
[Probability Calculator --> Discrete]{.fun}.
3. (Optional) In the `Calculation` textbox, type a name for the calculation 
(e.g., `Type I error`).  
4. In the [Distribution]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Binomial]{.fun}.
5. In the [No. of Trials]{.des} textbox, enter [15]{.typein}.
6. In the [Prob. of Success]{.des} textbox, enter [0.36]{.typein}.
7. In the next dropdown menu, select [Outside]{.dpd}, and in the textboxes 
next to it, enter [3]{.typein} and [8]{.typein}.
8. (Optional) Select the [Graph]{.des} checkbox to see a graph showing 
calculation details.
9. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) 
to see the result.
10. (Optional) In the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) textbox, enter 
the name [Type I Error Social]{.typein} to save this calculation to your 
Rguroo account.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout appearance="simple" collapse="true" 
icon="none" title="Click here to see the Rguroo dialog"}
![Probability calculator dialog for computing Type I error probability
](Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/TypeI_Error_social_dialog.png){width=500px}
:::
:::

@fig-youtube-type1c shows the Rguroo Probability Calculator output for 
$X \sim \text{Binomial}(15, 0.36)$, giving 

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = P(X \leq 3 \text{ or } X \geq 8 \mid p = 0.36) 
\approx 0.2833.$$
:::

That is, if the true proportion of students who use social media as their 
primary news source is $p = 0.36$, and our decision rule is to reject $H_0$ 
whenever $X \leq 3$ or $X \geq 8$ in a sample of $n = 15$ students, then there 
is approximately a $28.33\%$ chance of committing a Type I error; that is, 
incorrectly concluding that the true proportion of students who use social media 
as their primary news source differs from 0.36.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-cap: "Binomial distribution for $X \\sim \\text{Binomial}(n = 15, p = 0.36)$ showing the Type I error probability as the two-tail areas $P(X \\leq 3 \\text{ or } X \\geq 8) = 0.2833$."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-youtube-type1c
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/Type I Error social_graph.png")
```
::::

Now that we have computed the probability of a Type I error at various values 
of $p$, let us look back at what we observed in our examples.

We begin with the two-sided case. In the Social Media News example, $H_0$ 
specifies only a single value, $p = 0.36$, so there is only one possible 
value at which the probability of a Type I error can be computed. There is 
nothing further to compare in this case.

For the one-sided cases, however, there are many values of $p$ allowed by 
$H_0$, and we computed the probability of a Type I error at several of them. 
You may have noticed a pattern: in both the YouTube example and the Physical 
Inactivity example, the probability of a Type I error was largest at the value 
of $p$ where $H_0$ and $H_a$ meet, the **boundary value of $p$ in $H_0$**. 
For the YouTube example, this is $p = 0.70$, and for the Physical Inactivity example, this is $p = 0.35$. Is this a coincidence that the probability of a Type I error is largest at the boundary value?

It turns out that this is not a coincidence at all.

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Result: Maximum Type I Error Occurs at the Boundary Value
For the one-sided tests, where the critical region is in the direction of $H_a$, the probability of a Type I error is largest at the boundary value of $H_0$.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
A Type I error can occur only when $H_0$ is true. If the true value of $p$ is in $H_a$, then rejecting $H_0$ would be a correct decision, not a Type I error.
:::

@fig-type-range confirms this result for our two one-sided examples, showing 
the probability of a Type I error across a range of values of $p$ in $H_0$, 
for three different decision rules in each case.

In panel (a), for the YouTube example, the null hypothesis is 
$H_0: p \leq 0.70$, so the boundary value is $p = 0.70$. The three curves 
correspond to the decision rules $X \geq 16$, $X \geq 17$, and $X \geq 18$. 
For all three rules, the probability of a Type I error increases as $p$ gets 
closer to the boundary value of $0.70$, and reaches its maximum at $p = 0.70$. 
In panel (b), for the Physical Inactivity example, the null hypothesis is 
$H_0: p \geq 0.35$, so the boundary value is $p = 0.35$. The three curves 
correspond to the decision rules $X \leq 11$, $X \leq 12$, and $X \leq 13$. 
Again, for all three rules, the probability of a Type I error is largest at the 
boundary value $p = 0.35$ and decreases as $p$ moves away from it. 

Notice also that the horizontal dashed red line in each panel is drawn at $0.05$. This represents a commonly chosen upper bound for the probability of a Type I error — that is, the significance level $\alpha = 0.05$. As discussed in the previous sections, the decision rule (critical region) that we use is chosen to satisfy two requirements:

1. At the boundary value of $H_0$, the probability of a Type I error does not exceed the desired significance level $\alpha$.
2. Among all critical regions satisfying (1), we choose the one containing the largest number of $X$ values, so that the probability of a Type II error is as small as possible.

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-cap: 'Type I error probabilities for binomial tests as a function of $p$. Panel (a) shows $X \sim \text{Binomial}(20, p)$ with $P(X \geq 16, 17, 18)$; panel (b) shows $X \sim \text{Binomial}(50, p)$ with $P(X \leq 11, 12, 13)$. The red dashed line marks $\alpha = 0.05$. The gray dotted line shows the boundary of $H_0$.'
#| fig-alt: 'Two side-by-side line graphs showing how Type I error changes with the true proportion π. In panel (a), for a binomial model with n = 20, three curves show P(X ≥ 16), P(X ≥ 17), and P(X ≥ 18) increasing as π increases from 0 to 0.70, with values up to about 0.25. In panel (b), for n = 50, three curves show P(X ≤ 11), P(X ≤ 12), and P(X ≤ 13) decreasing as π increases from 0.35 to 1, with values up to about 0.12. A horizontal red dashed line at 0.05 marks the significance level in both plots.'
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-type-range
#| out-width: "90%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/TypeI_error_range.png")
```


### Obtaining Critical Regions and P-values Using Rguroo's Probability Calculator **{#sec-prob-calculator}

In this subsection, we revisit examples from earlier in the chapter, but instead of using Rguroo's hypothesis testing module, we compute critical values and p-values directly from the sampling distribution under $H_0$ using Rguroo's probability calculator. This approach makes visible the area-under-the-curve interpretation of both quantities.

#### Using Inverse Probability to Find the Critical Region **

While we could determine the critical region that satisfies our significance 
level by trial and error, testing different decision rules one at a time as we 
did above, there is a more direct approach. We can use what is called an 
**inverse probability function**. Rather than providing a value of $X$ and 
asking for the corresponding probability, an inverse probability function works 
in reverse: we provide a probability and it returns the corresponding value of 
$X$.

To find the critical region, we use the binomial distribution with the boundary value of $p$ in $H_0$ as the probability of success and $n$ as the number of trials, and we provide the significance level $\alpha$ as the probability. The inverse probability function then returns the value of $X$ that defines our critical region. Below, we show how this is done in Rguroo using its built-in probability calculator.

::: {#exm-youtube-rejection}
## YouTube Use: Finding the Critical Region

In the YouTube Use example (@exm-youtube-ppss), we are testing 
$H_0: p \leq 0.70$ versus $H_a: p > 0.70$, where $p$ is the true 
proportion of students in the district who use YouTube daily. Using a sample of $n = 20$ students and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$, find the critical region for this test.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_a: p > 0.70$, large values of $X$ 
support $H_a$, so we are looking for a critical region in the upper tail of the binomial distribution. We use Rguroo's Discrete Distributions Probability Calculator with the following settings (see @fig-youtube-rejection-dialog):

- Open the **Probability-Simulation** toolbox.
- Select [Probability $\Rightarrow$ Values]{.des} to use the inverse probability function.
- In the [Distribution]{.dpd} dropdown, set the distribution to _Binomial_.
- Set the [No. of Trials]{.des} to $n = 20$ and the [Prob. of Success]{.des} 
to $0.70$, which is the boundary value of $p$ in $H_0$.
- From the dropdown, select [Upper Tail]{.dpd} and enter [$0.05$]{.typein} as the significance level $\alpha$.
- Select the checkbox [Graph]{.des} to see a detailed output.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Discrete Distributions Probability Calculator configured for the YouTube example with Binomial distribution, 20 trials, probability of success 0.70, upper tail selected, and significance level 0.05 entered."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-youtube-rejection-dialog
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Settings for the YouTube Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/youtube-rejection-dialog.png")
```

The output, shown in @fig-youtube-rejection, gives
$$P(X \geq 17) = 0.1071 \geq 0.05 \quad \text{and} \quad P(X \geq 18) = 0.03548 \leq 0.05.$$

This means that the decision rule $X \geq 17$ exceeds our significance level, while the decision rule $X \geq 18$ satisfies it. Therefore, the critical region at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ is 
$$X \geq 18.$$ That is, if we observe 18 or more students in our sample of $n = 20$ who report daily YouTube use, we reject $H_0$ in favor of $H_a: p > 0.70$.

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Binomial distribution histogram for n = 20 and p = 0.70, with the critical region X greater than or equal to 18 highlighted in gold, showing that P(X greater than or equal to 17) = 0.1071 exceeds 0.05 and P(X greater than or equal to 18) = 0.03548 does not."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-youtube-rejection
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Output for the YouTube Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/YouTube_rejection.png")
```
:::

::: {#exm-inactivity-rejection}
## Physical Inactivity: Finding the Critical Region

In the Physical Inactivity example (@exm-physical-inactivity-error), we are 
testing $H_0: p \geq 0.35$ versus $H_a: p < 0.35$, where $p$ is the 
true proportion of adults in the population who are physically inactive. Using a sample of $n = 50$ adults and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$, find the critical region for this test.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_a: p < 0.35$, small values of $X$ 
support $H_a$, so we are looking for a critical region in the lower tail of 
the binomial distribution. We use Rguroo's Discrete Distributions Probability 
Calculator with the following settings (see @fig-inactivity-rejection-dialog), 
changing only the settings that differ from @exm-youtube-rejection:

- Set the [No. of Trials]{.des} to $n = 50$ and the [Prob. of Success]{.des} 
to $0.35$, which is the boundary value of $p$ in $H_0$.
- From the dropdown, select [Lower Tail]{.dpd} and enter [$0.05$]{.typein} as 
the significance level $\alpha$.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Discrete Distributions Probability Calculator configured for the Physical Inactivity example with Binomial distribution, 50 trials, probability of success 0.35, lower tail selected, and significance level 0.05 entered."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-inactivity-rejection-dialog
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Settings for the Physical Inactivity Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/inactive-rejection-dialog.png")
```

The output, shown in @fig-inactivity-rejection, gives
$$P(X \leq 12) = 0.06613 \geq 0.05 \quad \text{and} \quad 
P(X \leq 11) = 0.03423 \leq 0.05.$$

This means that the decision rule $X \leq 12$ exceeds our significance level, 
while the decision rule $X \leq 11$ satisfies it. Therefore, the rejection 
region at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ is 
$$X \leq 11.$$
That is, if we observe 11 or fewer inactive adults in our sample of $n = 50$, 
we reject $H_0$ in favor of $H_a: p < 0.35$.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Binomial distribution histogram for n = 50 and p = 0.35, with the critical region X less than or equal to 11 highlighted in gold, showing that P(X less than or equal to 12) = 0.06613 exceeds 0.05 and P(X less than or equal to 11) = 0.03423 does not."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-inactivity-rejection
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Output for the Physical Inactivity Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/inactive_rejection.png")
```
:::

::: {#exm-social-rejection}
## Social Media as a Primary News Source: Finding the Critical Region

In the Social Media as a Primary News Source example 
(@exm-social-media-news-error), we are testing $H_0: p = 0.36$ versus 
$H_a: p \neq 0.36$, where $p$ is the true proportion of students who use 
social media as their primary news source. Using a sample of $n = 15$ students and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$, find the critical region for this test.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_a: p \neq 0.36$, both unusually small and unusually large values of $X$ support $H_a$, so we are looking for a two-sided critical region. For a two-sided test at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, we split the significance level equally between the two tails, allocating $0.025$ to each tail. We use Rguroo's Discrete Distributions Probability Calculator with the following settings 
(see @fig-social-rejection-dialog), changing only the settings that differ 
from @exm-youtube-rejection:

- Set the [No. of Trials]{.des} to $n = 15$ and the [Prob. of Success]{.des} 
to $0.36$, which is the value of $p$ specified by $H_0$.
- From the dropdown, select [Outside Tails]{.dpd} and enter [$0.025$]{.typein} 
in each tail box.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Discrete Distributions Probability Calculator configured for the Social Media example with Binomial distribution, 15 trials, probability of success 0.36, Outside Tails selected, and 0.025 entered in each tail."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-social-rejection-dialog
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Settings for the Social Media Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/social-rejection-dialog.png")
```

The output, shown in @fig-social-rejection, gives
$$P(X \leq 2) + P(X \geq 9) = 0.1032 \geq 0.05,$$
with the additional note that
$$P(X \leq 2) = 0.05281, \quad P(X \geq 9) = 0.05038,$$
$$P(X \leq 1) = 0.01168, \quad P(X \geq 10) = 0.01545.$$

This means that the decision rule $X \leq 2$ or $X \geq 9$ exceeds our 
significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$. Moving one step further in each tail, the decision rule $X \leq 1$ or $X \geq 10$ satisfies our significance level since $P(X \leq 1) + P(X \geq 10) = 0.01168 + 0.01545 = 0.02713 \leq 0.05$. Therefore, the critical region at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ is $$X \leq 1 \quad \text{or} \quad X \geq 10.$$
That is, if we observe 1 or fewer, or 10 or more students in our sample of 
$n = 15$ who use social media as their primary news source, we reject $H_0$ 
in favor of $H_a: p \neq 0.36$.
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Binomial distribution histogram for n = 15 and p = 0.36, with the critical region X less than or equal to 2 and X greater than or equal to 9 highlighted in gold, showing that P(X less than or equal to 2) plus P(X greater than or equal to 9) = 0.1032 exceeds 0.05, with additional tail probabilities noted."
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-social-rejection
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Probability Calculator Output for the Social Media Critical Region Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/social_rejection.png")
```
:::

Now that we have determined the critical regions for our three examples, we 
can use them to make decisions based on observed sample data. Recall that the 
decision rule is simple: if $x_{\text{obs}}$ falls in the critical region, we reject 
$H_0$; otherwise, we fail to reject $H_0$.

:::{#exm-youtube-decision}
## YouTube Use: Making a Decision

In the YouTube Use example (@exm-youtube-ppss), we are testing 
$H_0: p \leq 0.70$ versus $H_a: p > 0.70$, where $p$ is the true 
proportion of students in the district who use YouTube daily, using a sample 
of $n = 20$ students and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$.

a. Suppose the observed number of students who report daily YouTube use is 
$x_{\text{obs}} = 17$. Make a decision and interpret it in the context of the problem.
b. Suppose instead that $x_{\text{obs}} = 19$. Make a decision and interpret it in 
the context of the problem.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
From @exm-youtube-rejection, the critical region at significance level 
$\alpha = 0.05$ is $X \geq 18$.

a. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 17 < 18$, the observed value does not fall in the rejection 
region. Therefore, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data do not provide 
sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that more than 
70% of students in the district use YouTube daily.

b. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 19 \geq 18$, the observed value falls in the rejection 
region. Therefore, we **reject $H_0$** in favor of $H_a: p > 0.70$. The 
data provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that 
more than 70% of students in the district use YouTube daily.
:::

:::{#exm-inactivity-decision}
## Physical Inactivity: Making a Decision

In the Physical Inactivity example (@exm-physical-inactivity-ppss), we are 
testing $H_0: p \geq 0.35$ versus $H_a: p < 0.35$, where $p$ is the 
true proportion of adults in the population who are physically inactive, using 
a sample of $n = 50$ adults and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$.

a. Suppose the observed number of inactive adults is $x_{\text{obs}} = 14$. Make a 
decision and interpret it in the context of the problem.
b. Suppose instead that $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$. Make a decision and interpret it in 
the context of the problem.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
From @exm-inactivity-rejection, the critical region at significance level 
$\alpha = 0.05$ is $X \leq 11$.

a. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 14 > 11$, the observed value does not fall in the critical region. Therefore, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data do not provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that fewer than 35% of adults in the population are physically inactive.

b. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 10 \leq 11$, the observed value falls in the rejection 
region. Therefore, we **reject $H_0$** in favor of $H_a: p < 0.35$. The 
data provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that fewer than 35% of adults in the population are physically inactive.
:::

:::{#exm-social-decision}
## Social Media as a Primary News Source: Making a Decision

In the Social Media as a Primary News Source example 
(@exm-social-media-news-ppss), we are testing $H_0: p = 0.36$ versus 
$H_a: p \neq 0.36$, where $p$ is the true proportion of students who use social media as their primary news source, using a sample of $n = 15$ students and a significance level of $\alpha = 0.05$.

a. Suppose the observed number of students who use social media as their 
primary news source is $x_{\text{obs}} = 7$. Make a decision and interpret it in the context of the problem.
b. Suppose instead that $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$. Make a decision and interpret it in 
the context of the problem.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
From @exm-social-rejection, the critical region at significance level 
$\alpha = 0.05$ is $X \leq 1$ or $X \geq 10$.

a. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 7$ does not satisfy $X \leq 1$ or $X \geq 10$, the 
observed value does not fall in the critical region. Therefore, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The data do not provide sufficient evidence at the 5% 
significance level to conclude that the proportion of students who use social media as their primary news source differs from 0.36.

b. Since $x_{\text{obs}} = 10 \geq 10$, the observed value falls in the rejection 
region. Therefore, we **reject $H_0$** in favor of $H_a: p \neq 0.36$. The data provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that the proportion of students who use social media as their primary news source differs from 0.36.
:::

#### Using Probability Calculator to Compute the p-Value: One-Sided Examples**

We now illustrate how to compute the p-value and use it to make a decision, 
starting with the two one-sided examples that we have seen.

:::{#exm-youtube-pvalue}
## YouTube Use: Computing the p-Value and Making a Decision

In the YouTube Use example (@exm-youtube-ppss), we are testing 
$H_0: p \leq 0.70$ versus $H_a: p > 0.70$, where $p$ is the true 
proportion of students in the district who use YouTube daily. A random sample of $n = 20$ students is selected and $x_{\text{obs}} = 17$ report daily YouTube use.

a. Obtain the $H_a$ support.
b. Compute the p-value.
c. At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, make a decision and state your 
conclusion in the context of the problem.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
a\. **Obtain the $H_a$ support.**

Since $H_a: p > 0.70$, values of $X$ that are at least as supportive of $H_a$ 
as $x_{\text{obs}} = 17$ are values greater than or equal to 17. Therefore the $H_a$ 
support is $\{17, 18, 19, 20\}$.

b\. **Compute the p-value.**

The p-value is computed at the boundary value $p_0 = 0.70$:
$$\text{p-value} = P(X \geq 17 \mid p = 0.70).$$

We compute this probability using Rguroo's Discrete Distributions Probability 
Calculator with $X \sim \text{Binomial}(20, 0.70)$.

@fig-youtube-pvalue shows the Rguroo dialog and the output, giving

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$\text{p-value} = P(X \geq 17 \mid p = 0.70) \approx 0.1071.$$
:::

::: {#fig-youtube-pvalue layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="top" fig-cap="Rguroo setup (left) and p-value output (right) for the YouTube example. "}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Probability Calculator dialog configured for the YouTube p-value example with Binomial distribution, 20 trials, probability of success 0.70, and upper tail selected."
#| fig-cap: Rguroo dialog for computing the p-value
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/youtube_pvalue_dialog.png")
```
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Binomial distribution histogram for n = 20 and p = 0.70, with the p-value support highlighted, showing P(X greater than or equal to 17) = 0.1071."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo output showing the p-value"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/youtube_pvalue.png")
```
:::

c\. **Make a decision.**

Since the p-value $0.1071 > \alpha = 0.05$, we fail to reject $H_0$. The data 
do not provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that 
more than 70% of students in the district use YouTube daily. Note that this 
conclusion is consistent with the critical region approach: $x_{\text{obs}} = 17$ 
does not fall in the critical region $X \geq 18$ determined in 
@exm-youtube-rejection.

::::

:::{#exm-inactivity-pvalue}
## Physical Inactivity: Computing the p-Value and Making a Decision

In the Physical Inactivity example (@exm-physical-inactivity-ppss), we are 
testing $H_0: p \geq 0.35$ versus $H_a: p < 0.35$, where $p$ is the 
true proportion of adults in the population who are physically inactive. A 
random sample of $n = 50$ adults is selected and $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$ are found to be physically inactive.

a. Obtain the $H_a$ support.
b. Compute the p-value.
c. At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, make a decision and state your 
conclusion in the context of the problem.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
a\. **Obtain the $H_a$ support.**

Since $H_a: p < 0.35$, values of $X$ that are at least as supportive of $H_a$ as $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$ are values less than or equal to 10. Therefore the $H_a$ support is $\{0, 1, \ldots, 10\}$.

b\. **Compute the p-value.**

The p-value is computed at the boundary value $p_0 = 0.35$:
$$\text{p-value} = P(X \leq 10 \mid p = 0.35).$$

We compute this probability using Rguroo's Discrete Probability 
Calculator with $X \sim \text{Binomial}(50, 0.35)$. @fig-inactivity-pvalue shows the Rguroo dialog setup (left panel) and the Rguroo output (right panel) giving

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$\text{p-value} = P(X \leq 10 \mid p = 0.35) \approx 0.0160.$$
:::

::: {#fig-inactivity-pvalue layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="bottom" fig-cap="Rguroo setup (left) and p-value output (right) for the Physical Inactivity example."}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Probability Calculator dialog configured for the Physical Inactivity p-value example with Binomial distribution, 50 trials, probability of success 0.35, and lower tail selected."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo dialog for computing the p-value"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/inactivity_pvalue_dialog.png")
```
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Binomial distribution histogram for n = 50 and p = 0.35, with the p-value support highlighted, showing P(X less than or equal to 10)."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo output showing the p-value"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/inactivity_pvalue.png")
```
:::

c\. **Make a decision.**

Since the p-value $0.0160 < \alpha = 0.05$, we reject $H_0$ in favor of 
$H_a: p < 0.35$. The data provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance 
level to conclude that fewer than 35% of adults in the population are physically 
inactive. Note that this conclusion is consistent with the critical region 
approach: $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$ falls in the critical region $X \leq 11$ determined 
in @exm-inactivity-rejection.

::::

**Computing the p-Value and Making a Decision: The Two-Sided Case**

The two-sided case requires a bit more care. Since $H_a: p \neq p_0$ does not specify a direction, evidence against $H_0$ can come from either tail of the distribution. Recall from Case 3 that a value $x$ is in the $H_a$ support if $P(X = x \mid p = p_0) \leq P(X = x_{\text{obs}} \mid p = p_0)$, that is, if it is at least as unlikely as $x_{\text{obs}}$ under $H_0$.

:::{#exm-social-pvalue}
## Social Media as a Primary News Source: Computing the p-Value and Making a Decision

In the Social Media as a Primary News Source example (@exm-social-media-news-ppss), we are testing $H_0: p = 0.36$ versus $H_a: p \neq 0.36$, where $p$ is the true proportion of students who use social media as their primary news source. A random sample of $n = 15$ students is selected and $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$ report using social media as their primary news source.

a. Obtain the $H_a$ support.
b. Compute the p-value.
c. At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, make a decision and state your 
conclusion in the context of the problem.
:::

:::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution
a\. **Obtain the $H_a$ support.**

Since $H_a: p \neq 0.36$, values of $X$ that are at least as supportive of 
$H_a$ as the observed value $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$ are those that are at least as unlikely 
as $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$ under $H_0$. That is, the $H_a$ support consists of $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$ 
and all values $x$ for which
$$
P(X = x \mid p = 0.36) \leq P(X = 9 \mid p = 0.36).
$$

Figure @fig-social-pvalue0 shows the Rguroo dialog and output used to compute 
$P(X = x \mid p = 0.36)$. The probability of the observed value is 
$P(X = 9 \mid p = 0.36) \approx 0.0349$. Therefore, any value of $x$ with 
probability less than or equal to $0.0349$ is included in the $H_a$ support, 
in addition to $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$.

From the graph, the $H_a$ support consists of $\{0, 1\}$ in the left tail and 
$\{9, 10, 11, \ldots, 15\}$ in the right tail.

Note that the graph is truncated at $X = 12$, since the probabilities for 
values greater than 12 are extremely small.

::: {#fig-social-pvalue0 layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="bottom" fig-cap="Rguroo setup (left) and Rguroo output for $P(X=9)$ (right) for the Social Media example."}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Probability Calculator dialog configured for the Social Media p-value example with Binomial distribution, 15 trials, probability of success 0.36, and probability of x = 9 computed."
#| fig-cap: 'Rguroo dialog'
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/social_pvalue0_dialog.png")
```
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: 'Binomial distribution histogram for n = 15 and p = 0.36,  showing the probabilty of $X = 9$'
#| fig-cap: 'Rguroo output'
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/social_pvalue0.png")
```
:::

b\. **Compute the p-value.**

The p-value is computed at the boundary value $p_0 = 0.36$. Using Rguroo’s 
Discrete Probability Calculator with $X \sim \text{Binomial}(15, 0.36)$, we 
compute the probability that $X$ falls in the $H_a$ support.

Recall that the $H_a$ support consists of the values $\{0, 1, 9, 10, 11, \ldots, 15\}$. 
Therefore, the p-value is the total probability of these values under $H_0$.

Figure @fig-social-pvalue shows the Rguroo output, giving

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$$
\text{p-value} = P\left(X \text{ is a value in the } H_a \text{ support} 
\mid p = 0.36\right) \approx 0.0621.
$$
:::

::: {#fig-social-pvalue layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="bottom" fig-cap="Rguroo setup (left) and Rguroo output (right) for computing the p-value for the Social Media example."}

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Probability Calculator dialog configured for the Social Media p-value example with Binomial distribution, 15 trials, probability of success 0.36, and probability of x <=0 and x>=9 computed."
#| fig-cap: 'Rguroo dialog'
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/social_pvalue_dialog.png")
```
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: 'Binomial distribution histogram for n = 15 and p = 0.36,  showing the p-value for the social media example$'
#| fig-cap: 'Rguroo output'
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/social_pvalue.png")
```
:::

c\. **Make a decision.**

Since the p-value $0.0621 > \alpha = 0.05$, we fail to reject $H_0$ in favor of $H_a: p \neq 0.36$. The data do not provide sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that the proportion of students who use social media as their primary news source differs from 0.36.

This conclusion is consistent with the critical region approach: the observed value $x_{\text{obs}} = 9$ does not fall in the critical region $X \leq 1$ or $X \geq 10$ determined in @exm-social-rejection.

::::


### Homework for Section @sec-closer-look-cr-pvalue {#sec-rr-hw}

For each of the following scenarios, answer these questions:

a. Write the null and alternative hypotheses using mathematical notation.
b. Use Rguroo's Probability Calculator to find the critical region at
the given significance level.
c. Does $x_{\text{obs}}$ fall in the critical region? Do you reject or fail to reject $H_0$?
d. Obtain the $H_a$ support.
e. Use Rguroo's Probability Calculator to compute the p-value.
f. Is the p-value smaller than the significance level? Do you reject or fail to reject $H_0$?
g. State your decision in the context of the problem.

---

1. **Arizona Marijuana Legalization**
In Problem 1 (Arizona Marijuana Legalization) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$ is
the true proportion of Arizona registered voters who support marijuana
legalization. Do Arizona registered voters differ from Colorado residents
in their support for marijuana legalization? The sample consisted of
$n = 779$ voters, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 390$ favored legalization.
Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

2. **Australian Unemployment Outlook**
In Problem 2 (Australian Unemployment Outlook) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$ is
the true proportion of Australians in November 1997 who believed
unemployment would increase. Did Australian sentiment about unemployment
shift between July and November of 1997? The sample consisted of
$n = 631$ Australians, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 284$ believed
unemployment would increase. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

3. **Arkansas Identity Theft Complaints**
In Problem 3 (Arkansas Identity Theft Complaints) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of consumer complaints in Arkansas that were for
identity theft. Was the proportion of identity theft complaints in Arkansas
greater than the national figure of 23%? The sample consisted of
$n = 3{,}482$ complaints, of which $x_{\text{obs}} = 1{,}601$ were for
identity theft. Use $\alpha = 0.01$.

4. **Alaska Identity Theft Complaints**
In Problem 4 (Alaska Identity Theft Complaints) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of consumer complaints in Alaska that were for
identity theft. Was Alaska's proportion of identity theft complaints less
than the national proportion of 23%? The sample consisted of
$n = 1{,}432$ complaints, of which $x_{\text{obs}} = 321$ were for
identity theft. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

5. **Kennedy Assassination Conspiracy Belief**
In Problem 5 (Kennedy Assassination Conspiracy Belief) of @sec-stage-hw,
$p$ is the true proportion of American adults in 2013 who believed there
was a conspiracy behind President Kennedy's assassination. Is there evidence
that American belief in a conspiracy behind President Kennedy's assassination
changed between 2001 and 2013? The sample consisted of $n = 1{,}039$
adults, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 634$ believed in a conspiracy.
Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

6. **Autism Spectrum Disorder in Arizona**
In Problem 6 (Autism Spectrum Disorder in Arizona) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of children in Arizona diagnosed with ASD. Is the
rate of ASD diagnosis among children in Arizona greater than the national
rate of 1 in 88 children? The sample consisted of $n = 32{,}601$ children,
of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 507$ were diagnosed with ASD. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

7. **Graduate Degree Attainment: GSS2024**
In Problem 7 (Graduate Degree Attainment: GSS2024) of @sec-stage-hw, $p$
is the true proportion of U.S. adults who hold a graduate degree. Does the GSS2024 data provide evidence that the proportion of U.S. adults who hold a graduate degree differs from the 15% reported by the U.S. Census Bureau?
The sample consisted of $n = 388$ adults, of whom $x_{\text{obs}} = 33$
reported a graduate degree as their highest level of education.
Use $\alpha = 0.05$.


## Large Sample Hypothesis Test for One Population Proportion {#sec-large-sample-one-prop}

In the previous section, we carried out hypothesis tests for a population
proportion using the binomial distribution. That approach is conceptually
clean and statistically exact: because $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p_0)$
under $H_0$, every probability we compute is precise. Generally,
the binomial method is the ideal choice.

However, there are good reasons to also learn an alternative approach based on large sample theory; that is a method that relies on the central limit theorem and applies to large samples. First, and most importantly, the method generalizes naturally to other inference problems, comparing two population proportions, or drawing inferences about population means, where an exact distribution like the binomial is no longer available. Second, the large sample method is the one most commonly reported
in published research, so understanding it is essential for reading and
interpreting results you will encounter in practice. Finally, because $X$ is a discrete random variable, the binomial method cannot always achieve the target significance level $\alpha$ exactly; when the actual Type I error rate is $\alpha$, the achived value may be somewhat less.
The large sample method does not have this discreteness issue, since it
uses the continuous normal distribution. For example, recall that in @exm-gss-marijuana, the GSS2024 marijuana legalization example, the binomial method produced an
exact significance level of 4.799% rather than the target 5%.

The large sample approach rests on the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**,
which you have already seen in Chapter ???. Recall that the CLT states that for
large samples, the sampling distribution of the sample mean is
approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the population
distribution. We now take a moment to see exactly why it applies to
sample proportions.

### A Proportion Is a Mean {#sec-prop-is-mean}

Suppose we record whether each individual in our sample has the
characteristic of interest, assigning a value of 1 to each success and a
value of 0 to each failure. For example, in the YouTube study, a student
who uses YouTube daily receives a 1, and one who does not receives a 0. If
our sample of $n = 20$ students produced $x_{\text{obs}} = 17$ daily users,
the data would consist of 17 ones and 3 zeros.

Now notice something: the **sample proportion** $\hat{p}$ is simply the
average of these zeros and ones:

$$
\hat{p}_{obs} = \frac{x_{\text{obs}}}{n}
        = \frac{\text{sum of zeros and ones}}{n}
        = \bar{x}.
$$

This means $\hat{p}_{obs}$ is a sample mean; specifically, the sample mean of a
collection of 0/1 values. Recall from the CLT that when the sample size is
large, the sampling distribution of a sample mean is approximately normal,
regardless of the shape of the original population distribution. Since
$\hat{p}$ is a sample mean, the CLT applies to it as well.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## A Proportion Is a Mean

The sample proportion $\hat{p}$ is the mean of a collection of zeros and
ones. Because the Central Limit Theorem applies to sample means when $n$
is large, it applies to $\hat{p}$ as well.
:::

More precisely, applying the CLT to $\hat{p}$ as a sample mean, for a
population with proportion of successes $p$, the sample proportion $\hat{p}$
has:

- **Mean:** $p$
- **Standard deviation:** $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$

This standard deviation is often called the **standard error** of $\hat{p}$.
It measures how much we expect $\hat{p}$ to vary from sample to sample. 
By the CLT, when $n$ is sufficiently large, $\hat{p}$
follows approximately a normal distribution:

$$
\hat{p} \sim N\!\left(p,\; \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\right).
$$

Since $p$ is unknown, we need to specify a value for it to use this
result for inference. Recall that in hypothesis testing we assume the null hypothesis is true, and that the maximum Type I error occurs at the
boundary value $p_0$ of $H_0$. We therefore evaluate the sampling
distribution of $\hat{p}$ at $p = p_0$, giving:

$$
\hat{p} \sim N\!\left(p_0,\; \sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}\right).
$$

We call this the **null distribution** of $\hat{p}$, since it describes
the behavior of $\hat{p}$ at the boundary value $p_0$ of $H_0$, where
the Type I error is at its maximum. Just as the
binomial null distribution of $X$ was the foundation for decisions in the
previous section, this normal null distribution of $\hat{p}$ is the
foundation for decisions in the large sample method. 

Most textbooks present the large sample test using a standardized
statistic called the $z$-score, obtained by subtracting $p_0$ from
$\hat{p}$ and dividing by the standard error:

$$
z = \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}}.
$$

This unit-less quantity is then compared to a standard normal
distribution. In this textbook, we instead work directly with $\hat{p}$
and compare it to its null distribution. This results in less
computation steps and, more importantly, keeps our decisions grounded in
the context of the data; we make conclusions about a sample proportion,
not an abstract standardized value.

Finally, what constitutes a large sample size wehere the normal approximation to the binomial is good? A common rule of thumb is to check the expected numbers of successes and failures. In particular, the approximation is typically considered reliable when
$np_0 \geq 10$ and n(1 − p_0) \geq 10$. When both of hese conditions are satisfied, the binomial distribution tends to be reasonably symmetric, and the normal model provides a good fit. A less strict guideline sometimes used is $np_0 \geq 5$ and $n(1 − p_0) \geq 5$, but this weaker condition can lead to noticeable inaccuracies, especially in tail probabilities. For this reason, the more conservative "10 and 10" rule is generally preferred in practice. 

To give examples, in the case of the YouTube Daily Use example, we have $n=20$ and $p_0=0.7$. Calculating the expected number of successes and failures, we get:

- Expected successes: $np_0 = 20 \times 0.7 = 14$
- Expected failures: $n(1 - p_0) = 20 \times (1 - 0.7) = 20 \times 0.3 = 6$

In this case, the expected number of successes (14) is greater than 10, but the expected number of failures (6) is less than 10 which suggests that the normal approximation may not be reliable for this scenario. The expected number of successes (16) is greater than 10, but since both conditions need to be satisfied for the normal approximation to be considered good, we would conclude that the normal approximation is not appropriate for this example. 

In contrast, for the Physical Inactivity example, we have $n=50$ and $p_0=0.35$. Calculating the expected number of successes and failures, we get:

- Expected successes: $np_0 = 50 \times 0.35 = 17.5$
- Expected failures: $n(1 - p_0) = 50 \times (1 - 0.35) = 50 \times 0.65 = 32.5$

In this case, both the expected number of successes (17.5) and the expected number of failures (32.5) are greater than 10, which suggests that the normal approximation is likely to be reliable for this scenario. Therefore, we can use the normal approximation to the binomial distribution for the Physical Inactivity example, but we should be cautious about using it for the YouTube Daily Use example due to the small expected number of successes.



::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Large Sample Condition: The 10 and 10 Rule

The normal approximation for $\hat{p}$ is considered reliable when both of
the following hold:

$$np_0 \geq 10 \qquad \text{and} \qquad n(1-p_0) \geq 10.$$

These conditions ensure that the sample is large enough for the CLT to
provide a good approximation. If either condition fails, the binomial
method from Section @sec-cr-pvalue-rguroo is the appropriate choice.
:::

### Decision Based on a Critical Region {#sec-large-sample-cr}

Recall from Section @sec-null-alternative that the critical region is a
set of values of our statistic that favor the alternative hypothesis, for
which we reject $H_0$. In the binomial method, the critical region was
expressed in terms of the count $X$. In the large sample method, we work
with $\hat{p}$ instead, and we find critical values using the normal null
distribution of $\hat{p}$.

For a one-sided test with $H_a: p > p_0$, large values of $\hat{p}$
favor the alternative, so the critical region lies in the right tail. We
find the critical value $\hat{p}^*$ such that
$P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}^*) = \alpha$ under the null distribution, and
reject $H_0$ if $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} \geq \hat{p}^*$, where $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ denotes the sample proportion that we have observed.

Analogously, for $H_a: p < p_0$, the critical region is in the left
tail, and we find $\hat{p}^*$ such that $P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}^*) = \alpha$.
For the two-sided test $H_a: p \neq p_0$, the critical region lies in
both tails, and we find lower and upper critical values $\hat{p}^*_L$ and
$\hat{p}^*_U$ such that $P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}^*_L) = \alpha/2$ and
$P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}^*_U) = \alpha/2$.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Critical Region for the Large Sample Test

All critical values are found using the null distribution
$\hat{p} \sim N\!\left(p_0,\, \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\right)$.

| Alternative Hypothesis | Critical Region | Critical Value Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $H_a: p > p_0$ | $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} \geq \hat{p}^*$ | $P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}^*) = \alpha$ |
| $H_a: p < p_0$ | $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} \leq \hat{p}^*$ | $P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}^*) = \alpha$ |
| $H_a: p \neq p_0$ | $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} \leq \hat{p}^*_L$ or $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} \geq \hat{p}^*_U$ | $P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}^*_L) = P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}^*_U) = \alpha/2$ |
:::

### Decision Based on a P-value {#sec-large-sample-pvalue}

As in the binomial method, we define the p-value as the probability that
$\hat{p}$ belongs to the $H_a$ support, assuming $H_0$ is true:

$$
\text{p-value} = P\!\left(\hat{p} \text{ belongs to } H_a \text{ support}
                  \mid H_0 \text{ is true}\right),
$$

where the $H_a$ **support** is the set of $\hat{p}$ values that are as
favorable or more favorable to $H_a$ than $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$, where $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ is the observed sample proportion. 

For one-sided tests this is straightforward: if
$H_a: p > p_0$, larger values of $\hat{p}$ are more favorable to
$H_a$, so the $H_a$ support consists of $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ and all
values above it. Symmetrically, for $H_a: p < p_0$, the $H_a$ support
consists of $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ and all values below it.

For the two-sided test $H_a: p \neq p_0$, the $H_a$ support consists
of values in both tails of the null distribution, symmetrically positioned about $p_0$ due to the normal distribution being symmetric about $p_0$ (see @fig-ha-support). If
$\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} > p_0$, the $H_a$ support is the set of values
$\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ together with their mirror image
relative to $p_0$ in the left tail. If $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} < p_0$,
the $H_a$ support is the set of values $\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$
together with their mirror image relative to $p_0$ in the right tail.
@fig-ha-support illustrates these two cases. In all cases, probabilities are computed using the normal null distribution.

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| label: fig-ha-support
#| fig-cap: "The $H_a$ support for a two-sided test. The shaded segments
#|   show the values of $\\hat{p}$ that are as favorable or more favorable
#|   to $H_a$ than $\\hat{p}_{\\text{obs}}$. The dashed tick marks the
#|   mirror image of $\\hat{p}_{\\text{obs}}$ relative to $p_0$."
#| fig-alt: "Two number lines illustrating the Ha support for a two-sided
#|   test. In Case 1 where p-hat-obs is greater than pi-0, the Ha support
#|   is highlighted in orange on the right tail beyond p-hat-obs and the
#|   left tail beyond its mirror image. In Case 2 where p-hat-obs is less
#|   than pi-0, the Ha support is highlighted in blue on the left tail
#|   beyond p-hat-obs and the right tail beyond its mirror image."
#| out-width: "85%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/ha_support_number_line.svg")
```
::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## P-value for the Large Sample Test

All probabilities are computed using the null distribution
$\hat{p} \sim N\!\left(p_0,\, \sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\right)$.

| Alternative Hypothesis | $H_a$ Support | P-value |
|---|---|---|
| $H_a: p > p_0$ | $\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ | $P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}})$ |
| $H_a: p < p_0$ | $\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ | $P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}})$ |
| $H_a: p \neq p_0$, if $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} > p_0$ | $\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ and its mirror relative to $p_0$ | $2 \times P(\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}})$ |
| $H_a: p \neq p_0$, if $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} < p_0$ | $\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}}$ and its mirror relative to $p_0$ | $2 \times P(\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}_{\text{obs}})$ |
:::

:::{#exm-inactivity-large-sample}
## Physical Inactivity Among Adults (continued)

Recall @exm-physical-inactivity-ppss. A national health agency hopes that
fewer than 35% of adults in its country are physically inactive. A random
sample of $n = 50$ adults was selected and $x_{\text{obs}} = 10$ were found
to be physically inactive. The hypotheses are:
$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p \geq 0.35 \\
H_a: p < 0.35
\end{cases}
$$

a. Check whether the conditions for the normal approximation hold.
b. Use Rguroo's Proportion Inference function to obtain an output that includes
both the critical region and the p-value, then answer the following:
   (i) What is the critical region, and in words, what is the decision rule?
   (ii) What is the $H_a$ support, and what is the p-value?
   (iii) Write a probability statement that describes the p-value.
c. In light of the observed data and the critical region and p-value, state
your conclusion in the context of the problem.

:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a\. To check the large sample condition, we compute the expected number of
successes and failures under $H_0$, using the boundary value $p_0 = 0.35$:

$$np_0 = 50 \times 0.35 = 17.5 \geq 10$$


$$n(1 - p_0) = 50 \times 0.65 = 32.5 \geq 10.$$

Both conditions are satisfied, so the normal approximation is appropriate
for this example.

b\. Click to expand the box below to see how to use the [Proportion Inference]{.fun}  function in Rguroo to answer this question.

::: {#prop-inference-inactivity .callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px style='vertical-align:middle;'} One-Population proportion hypothesis test - Physical Inactivity example"}

1. Open the **Analytics** toolbox in Rguroo.  

2. From the [Analysis]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Proportion Inference]{.fun} → [One Population]{.fun}. This opens the [One Population Proportion Inference]{.dialog} dialog.

3. In the **Data** section of the dialog, enter the following:
   - In the [Factor Label]{.des} textbox, type a label for the categorical variable. In Rguroo, a “factor” refers to a categorical variable. For this example, enter [Physically Inactive]{.typein}.
   - In the [Success Label]{.des} textbox, type [Yes]{.typein}. A success corresponds to a student who uses YouTube daily.
   - In the [Failure Label]{.des} textbox, type [No]{.typein}.
   - In the [Sample Size]{.des} textbox, type [50]{.typein}.
   - In the [\# of Successes]{.des} textbox, type [10]{.typein}.

4. In the **Test of Hypothesis** section of the dialog, enter the following:
   - In the [Alternative Hyp. p]{.dpd} dropdown, select [<]{.typein} and enter [0.35]{.typein}.
   - Select the [Large sample z (p=p0)]{.des} checkbox to use the large sample normal distribution method.
   - Leave the [Significance Level]{.des} textbox at the default value of [0.05]{.typein}.

5. Click the ![Details](Rguroo_Icons/details.png) button to open the [Advanced Features]{.dialog} dialog.
   - Open the [Confidence Interval and Test of Hypothesis]{.tab}.
   - In the **Graphs** section, select both [P-Value]{.des} and [Critical Region]{.des}. The default is [P-Value]{.des}; selecting [Critical Region]{.des} will also display the critical region.

6. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) to view the output.

7. Click the ![Save as](Rguroo_Icons/save_as.png) button and save the result as [PhysicalProp]{.typein}.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo One-Population Proportion dialogs"}

::: {#fig-inactivity-rguroo layout-ncol=2 layout-valign="top" fig-cap="(Left) Rguroo One-Population Proportion dialog for the Physical Inactivity example and (right) Advanced Features dialog."}
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo One-Population Proportion dialog configured for the Physical Inactivity Use example with sample size 50, observed count 10, hypothesized proportion 0.35, and left-tailed alternative."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo Basics dialog"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/prop-inference-inactivity_dialog.png")
```
```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo Advanced Features dialog for one population proportion inference showing the Confidence Interval and Test of Hypothesis tab. In the Methods section, the large sample z test with p = p0 option is checked. In the Graphs section, both P-Value and Critical Region options are selected and highlighted. At the bottom, Simulation Parameters show 10,000 replications with a seed value of 100."
#| fig-cap: "Advanced Features dialog"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/prop-inference-inactivity_advanced_dialog.png")
```
:::

:::

:::

Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: One-Population Proportion Inference - Physical Inactivity"}

<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/Physical_inactivity_ztest_docx/Physical_inactivity.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: One Population Proportion Hypothesis Test - Physical Inactivity Example">
  </iframe>
</div>

:::

   (i) From the Rguroo output, the critical region is
   $\hat{p} \leq \hat{p}^*$, where $\hat{p}^* = 0.23905$ (see @fig-inactivity-critical).

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "A bell-shaped curve labeled “Observed Data Scale” with significance level 5% displayed above. The horizontal axis represents the proportion of “Yes.” The left tail of the curve is shaded red to indicate the critical region. A green triangle marks the observed sample proportion at 0.20, which lies within the red shaded critical region. Below the axis, the critical value is shown as 0.23905."
#| label: fig-inactivity-critical
#| fig-cap: "A Portion of Rguroo Output: Critical Region Graph for the Physical Inactivity Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/CR_inactivity.png")
```

   In words: if the observed sample proportion of physically inactive adults
   is 0.23905 or less, we reject $H_0$ and conclude that
   fewer than 35% of adults in the country are physically inactive.

   (ii) Since $H_a: p < 0.35$, the $H_a$ support consists of all values of
   $\hat{p}$ that are less than or equal to the observed sample proportion
   $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} = 10/50 = 0.20$. From the Rguroo output, the
   p-value is approximately 0.01308 (see @fig-inactivity-pvalue).

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "A bell-shaped curve labeled “Observed Data Scale” with the horizontal axis showing the proportion of “Yes.” The left tail of the curve is shaded red to represent the p-value region. A green triangle marks the observed sample proportion at 0.20, located in the left tail. The legend indicates “Observed Sample Prop = 0.2” and “P-value = 0.013083."
#| label: fig-inactivity-pvalue
#| fig-cap: "A Portion of Rguroo Output: P-value Graph for the Physical Inactivity Example"
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/pvalue_inactivity.png")
```

   (iii) The p-value can be written as the probability
   $$\text{p-value} = P\!\left(\hat{p} \leq 0.20 \mid p = 0.35\right)
   \approx \text{0.01308}.$$
   This is the probability of observing a sample proportion as low as
   $0.20$ or lower, assuming that the true proportion of physically
   inactive adults is $p = 0.35$.

c\. Since the p-value (0.01308) is less than the significance level
    (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis $H_0$ and conclude that there is
    sufficient evidence to support the claim that fewer than 35% of adults in the country are physically inactive. 

    The same conclusion can be reached by comparing the observed sample proportion (0.20) to the critical value (0.23905). Since 0.20 is less than 0.23905, it falls within the critical region, leading us to reject $H_0$ and conclude that the data provide evidence in favor of the alternative hypothesis that the true proportion of physically inactive adults is less than 0.35.

:::


:::{#exm-gss-large-sample}
## Marijuana Legalization (continued)

Recall @exm-gss-marijuana. A researcher claims that more than 70% of
American adults support the legalization of marijuana. Using the
[GSS2024]{.data} dataset, which contains responses from $n = 388$
respondents, $x_{\text{obs}} = 282$ answered *Yes* to supporting
legalization. The hypotheses are:
$$
\begin{cases}
H_0: p \leq 0.70 \\
H_a: p > 0.70
\end{cases}
$$

a. Check whether the conditions for the normal approximation hold.
b. Use Rguroo's Proportion Inference function to obtain an output that includes
both the critical region and the p-value, then answer the following:
   (i) What is the critical region, and in words, what is the decision rule?
   (ii) What is the $H_a$ support, and what is the p-value?
   (iii) Write a probability statement that describes the p-value.
c\. In light of the observed data and the critical region and p-value, state
your conclusion in the context of the problem.

:::

::: {.callout-tip .solution-callout collapse="true" icon=false}
## 🔎 Solution

a\. To check the large sample condition, we compute the expected number of
successes and failures under $H_0$, using the boundary value $p_0 = 0.70$:

$$np_0 = 388 \times 0.70 = 271.6 \geq 10$$


$$n(1 - p_0) = 388 \times 0.30 = 116.4 \geq 10.$$

Both conditions are satisfied, so the normal approximation is appropriate
for this example.

b\. As in the [Physical Inactivity dialog](#exm-inactivity-rguroo),  we use the [Proportion Inference]{.fun} function. The dialog is shown in @fig-GSS2024_prop_normal_dialog. 

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "Rguroo One-Population Proportion dialog configured for the Marijuana Legalization example with sample size 388, observed count 282, hypothesized proportion 0.70, and right-tailed alternative."
#| fig-cap: "Rguroo One-Population Proportion dialog for the Marijuana Legalization example."
#| label: fig-GSS2024_prop_normal_dialog
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_dialogs/Proportion_inference/GSS2024_prop_normal_dialog.png")
```
Click to expand the box below to see the Rguroo output.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout collapse="true" title="Rguroo output: One-Population Proportion Inference - Marijuana Legalization"}
<div tabindex="0" class="rg-iframe-wrapper">
  <iframe
    src="Rguroo_html_output/Proportion Inference/GSS2024PropNormal_docx/GSS2024PropNormal.html"
    class="rg-iframe"
    title="Rguroo output: One Population Proportion Hypothesis Test - Marijuana Legalization Example">
  </iframe>
</div>
:::

   (i) From the Rguroo output, the critical region is $\hat{p} \geq \hat{p}^*$, where $\hat{p}^* = 0.73827$ (see @fig-GSS2024_critical).

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "A bell-shaped curve labeled “Observed Data Scale” with significance level 5% displayed above. The horizontal axis represents the proportion of “Yes.” The right tail of the curve is shaded red to indicate the critical region. A green triangle marks the observed sample proportion at 0.7268, which is just below the red shaded critical region. Below the axis, a vertical line indicates the critical value."
#| fig-cap: "A portion of Rguroo output: Critical Region Graph for the Marijuana Legalization Example"
#| label: fig-GSS2024_critical
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/GSS2024_prop_normal_critical.png")
``` 

   In words: if the observed sample proportion of adults who support
   legalization is 0.73827 or greater, we reject $H_0$ and
   conclude that more than 70% of American adults support the legalization
   of marijuana.

   (ii) Since $H_a: p > 0.70$, the $H_a$ support consists of all values of $\hat{p}$ that are greater than or equal to the observed sample proportion $\hat{p}_{\text{obs}} = 282/388 \approx 0.7268$. From the Rguroo output, the p-value is approximately 0.0.12463 (see @fig-GSS2024_pvalue).

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "A bell-shaped curve labeled “Observed Data Scale” with the horizontal axis showing the proportion of “Yes.” The right tail of the curve is shaded red to represent the p-value region. A green triangle marks the observed sample proportion at 0.7268, located just below the red shaded critical region. The legend indicates “Observed Sample Prop = 0.7268” and “P-value = 0.0625."
#| fig-cap: "A portion of Rguroo output: P-value Graph for the Marijuana Legalization Example"
#| label: fig-GSS2024_pvalue
#| out-width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics("Rguroo_outputs/Proportion_Inference/GSS2024_prop_normal_pvalue.png")
```

   (iii) The p-value can be written as the probability
   $$\text{p-value} = P\!\left(\hat{p} \geq 0.7268 \mid p = 0.70\right)
   \approx \text{0.12463}.$$
   This is the probability of observing a sample proportion as high as
   $0.7268$ or higher, assuming that the true proportion of adults who
   support legalization is $p = 0.70$.

c\. Since the p-value (0.12463) is greater than the significance level (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis $H_0$. Therefore, we do not have sufficient evidence to support the claim that more than 70% of American adults support the legalization of marijuana. In other words, based on the observed data, we cannot conclude that the true proportion of adults who support marijuana legalization exceeds 0.70.
